title: Building My First Power BI Dashboard

content:
Introduction

My journey into data analytics began while working in pension administration, where I was responsible for financial data reconciliation, reporting, and validating pension payment records. Although accuracy and compliance were key aspects of the role, I found myself particularly interested in analyzing data, identifying discrepancies, and uncovering patterns.

Discovering Analytics

A significant part of my work involved comparing datasets from banks against internal records, performing variance analysis in Excel, and preparing reports for management. These responsibilities strengthened my analytical thinking and attention to detail while showing me the value of data-driven decision-making.

Over time, I realized I wanted to deepen my analytical skills and work with more advanced tools that could transform raw data into actionable insights.

Building Technical Skills

To support this transition, I began learning:

  • SQL and MySQL for data querying and database management
  • Power BI for dashboard development and business reporting
  • Tableau for interactive data visualization
  • Advanced Excel for reporting and data manipulation
  • Python for data analysis and automation

I also started working on hands-on projects using real-world datasets to strengthen my practical experience.

Current Learning Path

I am currently pursuing a Diploma in Data Analytics & AI Specialist, where I continue to expand my knowledge of analytics, business intelligence, and emerging AI technologies.

Looking Ahead

My goal is to combine my business experience and analytical skills to help organizations make informed decisions through data. As I continue learning and building projects, I look forward to applying data analytics to solve real-world business challenges and create meaningful impact.
description: How my experience in pension administration inspired my transition into data analytics."
title: My Journey into Data Analytics

content:
Emoji can be enabled in a Hugo project in a number of ways. :zap:

Emoji Support

The emojify function can be called directly in templates or Inline Shortcodes.

To enable emoji globally, set enableEmoji to true in your site’s configuration and then you can type emoji shorthand codes directly in content files; e.g.

The Emoji cheat sheet is a useful reference for emoji shorthand codes.

N.B. The above steps enable Unicode Standard emoji characters and sequences in Hugo, however the rendering of these glyphs depends on the browser and the platform. To style the emoji you can either use a third party emoji font or a font stack; e.g.

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}

X Simple Shortcode

{{< youtube w7Ft2ymGmfc >}}


Theme Custom Shortcodes

These shortcodes are not Hugo built-ins, but are provided by the theme.

Responsive Images with Cloudinary

You can learn more about this here.

Set the cloudinary_cloud_name parameter in your site config to use this shortcode.

{{< dynamic-img src="/my/image/on/cloudinary" title="A title for the image" >}}

Note that you do not include the file extension (e.g. .png) in the src parameter, as the shortcode will automatically determine the best quality and format for the user’s device.

Optionally, you can customize the general CSS styles for the image:

{{< dynamic-img src="/my/image/on/cloudinary" title="A title for the image" style="max-width:60%" >}}
title: Rich Content

content:
Creating my first Power BI dashboard was an exciting milestone in my data analytics journey. It helped me turn raw data into meaningful insights and taught me how powerful visualization can be in storytelling. Building this dashboard was an eye‑opening experience into how global economic shifts impact companies, industries, and entire regions.

1. Project Overview

I built a World Layoffs - Insignts & Trends dashboard to analyze worldwide layoffs from 2020 to 2024, covering more than 1,000 companies, 31 industries, and nearly 485,000 employees affected. The goal of this project was to understand how layoffs evolved globally during and after the pandemic. I wanted to explore:

  • Which companies had the highest layoffs
  • Which industries were most affected
  • How layoffs changed year‑over‑year
  • Which regions experienced the largest impact
  • Monthly patterns and deep‑dive trends

Tool Used: Power BI Desktop
Dataset: Global Layoffs (2020–2024) — includes company details, industry, country, layoff counts, percentage laid off, funds raised, and timeline fields

2. Data Preparation

Before creating visuals, I performed data cleaning and transformation using Power Query Editor, ensuring accuracy and consistancy before modeling.

Key Steps Included:

  • Removing duplicates and null values
  • Standardizing industry and country names
  • Converting date fields into proper formats
  • Creating Year, Month, and Quarter columns
  • Adding calculated fields for YoY change and total layoffs
  • Ensuring numeric fields (layoff count, funds raised) were properly typed

3. Dashboard Design

![Dashboard Design Screenshot](/images/World Layoffs - Insignts & Trends dashboard.png)

I designed a two‑page dashboard to separate high‑level insights from deeper analysis.

Page 1: Global Layoff Overview

Focused on global patterns and top‑level metrics.

Key visuals:

  • KPI Cards: Total layoffs, average layoff %, max single-company layoff
  • Top 10 Companies by Layoffs
  • Industry‑Wise Layoffs (Pie Chart)
  • Yearly Totals + YoY Change
  • Global Map (Layoffs by Country)

Page 2: Detailed Layoff Insights & Deep Dive

This page explores detailed patterns and company‑level insights.

Key visuals:

  • Companies with 100% Layoffs (shutdowns)
  • Layoffs by Country & Industry (stacked bar)
  • Monthly Layoffs Trend (2020–2024)

My goal was a clean, intuitive layout that allows users to move from a global view to detailed insights seamlessly.

4. Key Insights

Tech and Retail were the hardest hit

Tech companies like Amazon, Tesla, Dell, Google, Meta, and SAP dominated the Top 10 list. Retail and Transportation also showed significant layoffs due to shifting consumer behavior and supply chain disruptions.

Layoffs peaked in 2023

The YoY chart revealed a sharp spike in 2023 — the highest in the dataset. 2024 showed early signs of stabilization.

Several companies shut down completely

The “100% Layoffs” table highlighted companies that ceased operations, including:

  • Katerra (Construction)
  • Bitwise (Tech)
  • Zulily (Retail)
  • Convoy (Logistics)

These cases often involved startups with high fundraising but unsustainable cost structures.

North America and Europe saw the largest impact

The map visualization showed the highest concentration of layoffs in the US, Canada, UK, and Germany.

Monthly trends showed seasonal patterns

Layoffs often spiked in Q1, aligning with annual restructuring cycles.

5. Challenges Faced

Like any real‑world project, this one came with its own learning curve:

  • Understanding how to model multiple dimensions (Company, Industry, Country, Date)
  • Choosing visuals that clearly communicate trends
  • Designing a layout that works across two pages
  • Syncing slicers across pages for a smooth user experience
  • Ensuring the dashboard remained clean despite large amounts of data

Each challenge helped me improve my BI design and analytical thinking.

Blockquotes

The blockquote element represents content that is quoted from another source, optionally with a citation which must be within a footer or cite element, and optionally with in-line changes such as annotations and abbreviations.

Blockquote without attribution

Tiam, ad mint andaepu dandae nostion secatur sequo quae. Note that you can use Markdown syntax within a blockquote.

Blockquote with attribution

Don’t communicate by sharing memory, share memory by communicating.

— Rob Pike1

Tables

Tables aren’t part of the core Markdown spec, but Hugo supports supports them out-of-the-box.

  </head>

content:
Creating my first Power BI dashboard was an exciting milestone in my data analytics journey. It helped me turn raw data into meaningful insights and taught me how powerful visualization can be in storytelling. Building this dashboard was an eye‑opening experience into how global economic shifts impact companies, industries, and entire regions.

1. Project Overview

I built a World Layoffs - Insignts & Trends dashboard to analyze worldwide layoffs from 2020 to 2024, covering more than 1,000 companies, 31 industries, and nearly 485,000 employees affected. The goal of this project was to understand how layoffs evolved globally during and after the pandemic. I wanted to explore:

  • Which companies had the highest layoffs
  • Which industries were most affected
  • How layoffs changed year‑over‑year
  • Which regions experienced the largest impact
  • Monthly patterns and deep‑dive trends

Tool Used: Power BI Desktop
Dataset: Global Layoffs (2020–2024) — includes company details, industry, country, layoff counts, percentage laid off, funds raised, and timeline fields

2. Data Preparation

Before creating visuals, I performed data cleaning and transformation using Power Query Editor, ensuring accuracy and consistancy before modeling.

Key Steps Included:

  • Removing duplicates and null values
  • Standardizing industry and country names
  • Converting date fields into proper formats
  • Creating Year, Month, and Quarter columns
  • Adding calculated fields for YoY change and total layoffs
  • Ensuring numeric fields (layoff count, funds raised) were properly typed

3. Dashboard Design

I designed a two‑page dashboard to separate high‑level insights from deeper analysis.

Page 1: Global Layoff Overview

Focused on global patterns and top‑level metrics.

Key visuals:

  • KPI Cards: Total layoffs, average layoff %, max single-company layoff
  • Top 10 Companies by Layoffs
  • Industry‑Wise Layoffs (Pie Chart)
  • Yearly Totals + YoY Change
  • Global Map (Layoffs by Country)

Page 2: Detailed Layoff Insights & Deep Dive

This page explores detailed patterns and company‑level insights.

Key visuals:

  • Companies with 100% Layoffs (shutdowns)
  • Layoffs by Country & Industry (stacked bar)
  • Monthly Layoffs Trend (2020–2024)

My goal was a clean, intuitive layout that allows users to move from a global view to detailed insights seamlessly.

4. Key Insights

Tech and Retail were the hardest hit

Tech companies like Amazon, Tesla, Dell, Google, Meta, and SAP dominated the Top 10 list. Retail and Transportation also showed significant layoffs due to shifting consumer behavior and supply chain disruptions.

Layoffs peaked in 2023

The YoY chart revealed a sharp spike in 2023 — the highest in the dataset. 2024 showed early signs of stabilization.

Several companies shut down completely

The “100% Layoffs” table highlighted companies that ceased operations, including:

  • Katerra (Construction)
  • Bitwise (Tech)
  • Zulily (Retail)
  • Convoy (Logistics)

These cases often involved startups with high fundraising but unsustainable cost structures.

North America and Europe saw the largest impact

The map visualization showed the highest concentration of layoffs in the US, Canada, UK, and Germany.

Monthly trends showed seasonal patterns

Layoffs often spiked in Q1, aligning with annual restructuring cycles.

5. Challenges Faced

Like any real‑world project, this one came with its own learning curve:

  • Understanding how to model multiple dimensions (Company, Industry, Country, Date)
  • Choosing visuals that clearly communicate trends
  • Designing a layout that works across two pages
  • Syncing slicers across pages for a smooth user experience
  • Ensuring the dashboard remained clean despite large amounts of data

Each challenge helped me improve my BI design and analytical thinking.

6. What I Learned

This project strengthened my skills in:

  • Data cleaning and transformation using Power Query
  • Data modeling with a star schema
  • DAX calculations for YoY metrics and KPIs
  • Dashboard storytelling — turning raw data into meaningful insights
  • Designing multi‑page Power BI reports
  • Creating interactive visuals that support deeper exploration

Most importantly, I learned how to structure a dashboard that feels professional, intuitive, and portfolio‑ready.

title: Building My First Power BI Dashboard

content:
Introduction

My journey into data analytics began while working in pension administration, where I was responsible for financial data reconciliation, reporting, and validating pension payment records. Although accuracy and compliance were key aspects of the role, I found myself particularly interested in analyzing data, identifying discrepancies, and uncovering patterns.

Discovering Analytics

A significant part of my work involved comparing datasets from banks against internal records, performing variance analysis in Excel, and preparing reports for management. These responsibilities strengthened my analytical thinking and attention to detail while showing me the value of data-driven decision-making.

Over time, I realized I wanted to deepen my analytical skills and work with more advanced tools that could transform raw data into actionable insights.

Building Technical Skills

To support this transition, I began learning:

  • SQL and MySQL for data querying and database management
  • Power BI for dashboard development and business reporting
  • Tableau for interactive data visualization
  • Advanced Excel for reporting and data manipulation
  • Python for data analysis and automation

I also started working on hands-on projects using real-world datasets to strengthen my practical experience.

Current Learning Path

I am currently pursuing a Diploma in Data Analytics & AI Specialist, where I continue to expand my knowledge of analytics, business intelligence, and emerging AI technologies.

Looking Ahead

My goal is to combine my business experience and analytical skills to help organizations make informed decisions through data. As I continue learning and building projects, I look forward to applying data analytics to solve real-world business challenges and create meaningful impact.
description: How my experience in pension administration inspired my transition into data analytics."
title: My Journey into Data Analytics

title: Rich Content

content:
Creating my first Power BI dashboard was an exciting milestone in my data analytics journey. It helped me turn raw data into meaningful insights and taught me how powerful visualization can be in storytelling. Building this dashboard was an eye‑opening experience into how global economic shifts impact companies, industries, and entire regions.

1. Project Overview

I built a World Layoffs - Insignts & Trends dashboard to analyze worldwide layoffs from 2020 to 2024, covering more than 1,000 companies, 31 industries, and nearly 485,000 employees affected. The goal of this project was to understand how layoffs evolved globally during and after the pandemic. I wanted to explore:

  • Which companies had the highest layoffs
  • Which industries were most affected
  • How layoffs changed year‑over‑year
  • Which regions experienced the largest impact
  • Monthly patterns and deep‑dive trends

Tool Used: Power BI Desktop
Dataset: Global Layoffs (2020–2024) — includes company details, industry, country, layoff counts, percentage laid off, funds raised, and timeline fields

2. Data Preparation

Before creating visuals, I performed data cleaning and transformation using Power Query Editor, ensuring accuracy and consistancy before modeling.

Key Steps Included:

  • Removing duplicates and null values
  • Standardizing industry and country names
  • Converting date fields into proper formats
  • Creating Year, Month, and Quarter columns
  • Adding calculated fields for YoY change and total layoffs
  • Ensuring numeric fields (layoff count, funds raised) were properly typed

3. Dashboard Design

I designed a two‑page dashboard to separate high‑level insights from deeper analysis.

Page 1: Global Layoff Overview

Focused on global patterns and top‑level metrics.

Key visuals:

  • KPI Cards: Total layoffs, average layoff %, max single-company layoff
  • Top 10 Companies by Layoffs
  • Industry‑Wise Layoffs (Pie Chart)
  • Yearly Totals + YoY Change
  • Global Map (Layoffs by Country)

Page 2: Detailed Layoff Insights & Deep Dive

This page explores detailed patterns and company‑level insights.

Key visuals:

  • Companies with 100% Layoffs (shutdowns)
  • Layoffs by Country & Industry (stacked bar)
  • Monthly Layoffs Trend (2020–2024)

My goal was a clean, intuitive layout that allows users to move from a global view to detailed insights seamlessly.

4. Key Insights

Tech and Retail were the hardest hit

Tech companies like Amazon, Tesla, Dell, Google, Meta, and SAP dominated the Top 10 list. Retail and Transportation also showed significant layoffs due to shifting consumer behavior and supply chain disruptions.

Layoffs peaked in 2023

The YoY chart revealed a sharp spike in 2023 — the highest in the dataset. 2024 showed early signs of stabilization.

Several companies shut down completely

The “100% Layoffs” table highlighted companies that ceased operations, including:

  • Katerra (Construction)
  • Bitwise (Tech)
  • Zulily (Retail)
  • Convoy (Logistics)

These cases often involved startups with high fundraising but unsustainable cost structures.

North America and Europe saw the largest impact

The map visualization showed the highest concentration of layoffs in the US, Canada, UK, and Germany.

Monthly trends showed seasonal patterns

Layoffs often spiked in Q1, aligning with annual restructuring cycles.

5. Challenges Faced

Like any real‑world project, this one came with its own learning curve:

  • Understanding how to model multiple dimensions (Company, Industry, Country, Date)
  • Choosing visuals that clearly communicate trends
  • Designing a layout that works across two pages
  • Syncing slicers across pages for a smooth user experience
  • Ensuring the dashboard remained clean despite large amounts of data

Each challenge helped me improve my BI design and analytical thinking.

6. What I Learned

This project strengthened my skills in:

  • Data cleaning and transformation using Power Query
  • Data modeling with a star schema
  • DAX calculations for YoY metrics and KPIs
  • Dashboard storytelling — turning raw data into meaningful insights
  • Designing multi‑page Power BI reports
  • Creating interactive visuals that support deeper exploration

Most importantly, I learned how to structure a dashboard that feels professional, intuitive, and portfolio‑ready.

Final Thoughts

The World Layoffs dashboard helped me understand how global events ripple across industries and regions. It also allowed me to demonstrate my ability to work with complex datasets, build insightful visuals, and communicate findings clearly.

This project reflects my passion for data storytelling and my commitment to building dashboards that deliver real value.
title: Building My First Power BI Dashboard

content:
Introduction

My journey into data analytics began while working in pension administration, where I was responsible for financial data reconciliation, reporting, and validating pension payment records. Although accuracy and compliance were key aspects of the role, I found myself particularly interested in analyzing data, identifying discrepancies, and uncovering patterns.

Discovering Analytics

A significant part of my work involved comparing datasets from banks against internal records, performing variance analysis in Excel, and preparing reports for management. These responsibilities strengthened my analytical thinking and attention to detail while showing me the value of data-driven decision-making.

Over time, I realized I wanted to deepen my analytical skills and work with more advanced tools that could transform raw data into actionable insights.

Building Technical Skills

To support this transition, I began learning:

  • SQL and MySQL for data querying and database management
  • Power BI for dashboard development and business reporting
  • Tableau for interactive data visualization
  • Advanced Excel for reporting and data manipulation
  • Python for data analysis and automation

I also started working on hands-on projects using real-world datasets to strengthen my practical experience.

Current Learning Path

I am currently pursuing a Diploma in Data Analytics & AI Specialist, where I continue to expand my knowledge of analytics, business intelligence, and emerging AI technologies.

Looking Ahead

My goal is to combine my business experience and analytical skills to help organizations make informed decisions through data. As I continue learning and building projects, I look forward to applying data analytics to solve real-world business challenges and create meaningful impact.
description: How my experience in pension administration inspired my transition into data analytics."
title: My Journey into Data Analytics

content:
Emoji can be enabled in a Hugo project in a number of ways. :zap:

Emoji Support

The emojify function can be called directly in templates or Inline Shortcodes.

To enable emoji globally, set enableEmoji to true in your site’s configuration and then you can type emoji shorthand codes directly in content files; e.g.

The Emoji cheat sheet is a useful reference for emoji shorthand codes.

N.B. The above steps enable Unicode Standard emoji characters and sequences in Hugo, however the rendering of these glyphs depends on the browser and the platform. To style the emoji you can either use a third party emoji font or a font stack; e.g.

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}
title: Emoji Support

title: Rich Content

content:
Introduction

Creating my first Power BI dashboard was an exciting milestone in my data analytics journey. It helped me turn raw data into meaningful insights and taught me how powerful visualization can be in storytelling. Building this dashboard was an eye‑opening experience into how global economic shifts impact companies, industries, and entire regions.

1. Project Overview

I built a World Layoffs - Insignts & Trends dashboard to analyze worldwide layoffs from 2020 to 2024, covering more than 1,000 companies, 31 industries, and nearly 485,000 employees affected. The goal of this project was to understand how layoffs evolved globally during and after the pandemic. I wanted to explore:

  • Which companies had the highest layoffs
  • Which industries were most affected
  • How layoffs changed year‑over‑year
  • Which regions experienced the largest impact
  • Monthly patterns and deep‑dive trends

Tool Used: Power BI Desktop
Dataset: Global Layoffs (2020–2024) — includes company details, industry, country, layoff counts, percentage laid off, funds raised, and timeline fields

2. Data Preparation

Before creating visuals, I performed data cleaning and transformation using Power Query Editor, ensuring accuracy and consistancy before modeling.

Key Steps Included:

  • Removing duplicates and null values
  • Standardizing industry and country names
  • Converting date fields into proper formats
  • Creating Year, Month, and Quarter columns
  • Adding calculated fields for YoY change and total layoffs
  • Ensuring numeric fields (layoff count, funds raised) were properly typed

3. Dashboard Design

I designed a two‑page dashboard to separate high‑level insights from deeper analysis.

Page 1: Global Layoff Overview

Focused on global patterns and top‑level metrics.

Key visuals:

  • KPI Cards: Total layoffs, average layoff %, max single-company layoff
  • Top 10 Companies by Layoffs
  • Industry‑Wise Layoffs (Pie Chart)
  • Yearly Totals + YoY Change
  • Global Map (Layoffs by Country)

Page 2: Detailed Layoff Insights & Deep Dive

This page explores detailed patterns and company‑level insights.

Key visuals:

  • Companies with 100% Layoffs (shutdowns)
  • Layoffs by Country & Industry (stacked bar)
  • Monthly Layoffs Trend (2020–2024)

My goal was a clean, intuitive layout that allows users to move from a global view to detailed insights seamlessly.

4. Key Insights

Tech and Retail were the hardest hit

Tech companies like Amazon, Tesla, Dell, Google, Meta, and SAP dominated the Top 10 list. Retail and Transportation also showed significant layoffs due to shifting consumer behavior and supply chain disruptions.

Layoffs peaked in 2023

The YoY chart revealed a sharp spike in 2023 — the highest in the dataset. 2024 showed early signs of stabilization.

Several companies shut down completely

The “100% Layoffs” table highlighted companies that ceased operations, including:

  • Katerra (Construction)
  • Bitwise (Tech)
  • Zulily (Retail)
  • Convoy (Logistics)

These cases often involved startups with high fundraising but unsustainable cost structures.

North America and Europe saw the largest impact

The map visualization showed the highest concentration of layoffs in the US, Canada, UK, and Germany.

Monthly trends showed seasonal patterns

Layoffs often spiked in Q1, aligning with annual restructuring cycles.

5. Challenges Faced

Like any real‑world project, this one came with its own learning curve:

  • Understanding how to model multiple dimensions (Company, Industry, Country, Date)
  • Choosing visuals that clearly communicate trends
  • Designing a layout that works across two pages
  • Syncing slicers across pages for a smooth user experience
  • Ensuring the dashboard remained clean despite large amounts of data

Each challenge helped me improve my BI design and analytical thinking.

6. What I Learned

This project strengthened my skills in:

  • Data cleaning and transformation using Power Query
  • Data modeling with a star schema
  • DAX calculations for YoY metrics and KPIs
  • Dashboard storytelling — turning raw data into meaningful insights
  • Designing multi‑page Power BI reports
  • Creating interactive visuals that support deeper exploration

Most importantly, I learned how to structure a dashboard that feels professional, intuitive, and portfolio‑ready.

Final Thoughts

The World Layoffs dashboard helped me understand how global events ripple across industries and regions. It also allowed me to demonstrate my ability to work with complex datasets, build insightful visuals, and communicate findings clearly.

This project reflects my passion for data storytelling and my commitment to building dashboards that deliver real value.
title: Building My First Power BI Dashboard

content:
Introduction

My journey into data analytics began while working in pension administration, where I was responsible for financial data reconciliation, reporting, and validating pension payment records. Although accuracy and compliance were key aspects of the role, I found myself particularly interested in analyzing data, identifying discrepancies, and uncovering patterns.

Discovering Analytics

A significant part of my work involved comparing datasets from banks against internal records, performing variance analysis in Excel, and preparing reports for management. These responsibilities strengthened my analytical thinking and attention to detail while showing me the value of data-driven decision-making.

Over time, I realized I wanted to deepen my analytical skills and work with more advanced tools that could transform raw data into actionable insights.

Building Technical Skills

To support this transition, I began learning:

  • SQL and MySQL for data querying and database management
  • Power BI for dashboard development and business reporting
  • Tableau for interactive data visualization
  • Advanced Excel for reporting and data manipulation
  • Python for data analysis and automation

I also started working on hands-on projects using real-world datasets to strengthen my practical experience.

Current Learning Path

I am currently pursuing a Diploma in Data Analytics & AI Specialist, where I continue to expand my knowledge of analytics, business intelligence, and emerging AI technologies.

Looking Ahead

My goal is to combine my business experience and analytical skills to help organizations make informed decisions through data. As I continue learning and building projects, I look forward to applying data analytics to solve real-world business challenges and create meaningful impact.
description: How my experience in pension administration inspired my transition into data analytics."
title: My Journey into Data Analytics

content:
Emoji can be enabled in a Hugo project in a number of ways. :zap:

Emoji Support

The emojify function can be called directly in templates or Inline Shortcodes.

To enable emoji globally, set enableEmoji to true in your site’s configuration and then you can type emoji shorthand codes directly in content files; e.g.

The Emoji cheat sheet is a useful reference for emoji shorthand codes.

N.B. The above steps enable Unicode Standard emoji characters and sequences in Hugo, however the rendering of these glyphs depends on the browser and the platform. To style the emoji you can either use a third party emoji font or a font stack; e.g.

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}
title: Emoji Support

content:
First Step Towards Visualization

Creating my first Power BI dashboard was an exciting milestone in my data analytics journey. It helped me turn raw data into meaningful insights and taught me how powerful visualization can be in storytelling. Building this dashboard was an eye‑opening experience into how global economic shifts impact companies, industries, and entire regions.

1. Project Overview

I built a World Layoffs - Insignts & Trends dashboard to analyze worldwide layoffs from 2020 to 2024, covering more than 1,000 companies, 31 industries, and nearly 485,000 employees affected. The goal of this project was to understand how layoffs evolved globally during and after the pandemic. I wanted to explore:

  • Which companies had the highest layoffs
  • Which industries were most affected
  • How layoffs changed year‑over‑year
  • Which regions experienced the largest impact
  • Monthly patterns and deep‑dive trends

Tool Used: Power BI Desktop
Dataset: Global Layoffs (2020–2024) — includes company details, industry, country, layoff counts, percentage laid off, funds raised, and timeline fields

2. Data Preparation

Before creating visuals, I performed data cleaning and transformation using Power Query Editor, ensuring accuracy and consistancy before modeling.

Key Steps Included:

  • Removing duplicates and null values
  • Standardizing industry and country names
  • Converting date fields into proper formats
  • Creating Year, Month, and Quarter columns
  • Adding calculated fields for YoY change and total layoffs
  • Ensuring numeric fields (layoff count, funds raised) were properly typed

3. Dashboard Design

I designed a two‑page dashboard to separate high‑level insights from deeper analysis.

Page 1: Global Layoff Overview

Focused on global patterns and top‑level metrics.

Key visuals:

  • KPI Cards: Total layoffs, average layoff %, max single-company layoff
  • Top 10 Companies by Layoffs
  • Industry‑Wise Layoffs (Pie Chart)
  • Yearly Totals + YoY Change
  • Global Map (Layoffs by Country)

Page 2: Detailed Layoff Insights & Deep Dive

This page explores detailed patterns and company‑level insights.

Key visuals:

  • Companies with 100% Layoffs (shutdowns)
  • Layoffs by Country & Industry (stacked bar)
  • Monthly Layoffs Trend (2020–2024)

My goal was a clean, intuitive layout that allows users to move from a global view to detailed insights seamlessly.

4. Key Insights

Tech and Retail were the hardest hit

Tech companies like Amazon, Tesla, Dell, Google, Meta, and SAP dominated the Top 10 list. Retail and Transportation also showed significant layoffs due to shifting consumer behavior and supply chain disruptions.

Layoffs peaked in 2023

The YoY chart revealed a sharp spike in 2023 — the highest in the dataset. 2024 showed early signs of stabilization.

Several companies shut down completely

The “100% Layoffs” table highlighted companies that ceased operations, including:

  • Katerra (Construction)
  • Bitwise (Tech)
  • Zulily (Retail)
  • Convoy (Logistics)

These cases often involved startups with high fundraising but unsustainable cost structures.

North America and Europe saw the largest impact

The map visualization showed the highest concentration of layoffs in the US, Canada, UK, and Germany.

Monthly trends showed seasonal patterns

Layoffs often spiked in Q1, aligning with annual restructuring cycles.

5. Challenges Faced

Like any real‑world project, this one came with its own learning curve:

  • Understanding how to model multiple dimensions (Company, Industry, Country, Date)
  • Choosing visuals that clearly communicate trends
  • Designing a layout that works across two pages
  • Syncing slicers across pages for a smooth user experience
  • Ensuring the dashboard remained clean despite large amounts of data

Each challenge helped me improve my BI design and analytical thinking.

6. What I Learned

This project strengthened my skills in:

  • Data cleaning and transformation using Power Query
  • Data modeling with a star schema
  • DAX calculations for YoY metrics and KPIs
  • Dashboard storytelling — turning raw data into meaningful insights
  • Designing multi‑page Power BI reports
  • Creating interactive visuals that support deeper exploration

Most importantly, I learned how to structure a dashboard that feels professional, intuitive, and portfolio‑ready.

Final Thoughts

The World Layoffs dashboard helped me understand how global events ripple across industries and regions. It also allowed me to demonstrate my ability to work with complex datasets, build insightful visuals, and communicate findings clearly.

This project reflects my passion for data storytelling and my commitment to building dashboards that deliver real value.
title: Building My First Power BI Dashboard

content:
Introduction

My journey into data analytics began while working in pension administration, where I was responsible for financial data reconciliation, reporting, and validating pension payment records. Although accuracy and compliance were key aspects of the role, I found myself particularly interested in analyzing data, identifying discrepancies, and uncovering patterns.

Discovering Analytics

A significant part of my work involved comparing datasets from banks against internal records, performing variance analysis in Excel, and preparing reports for management. These responsibilities strengthened my analytical thinking and attention to detail while showing me the value of data-driven decision-making.

Over time, I realized I wanted to deepen my analytical skills and work with more advanced tools that could transform raw data into actionable insights.

Building Technical Skills

To support this transition, I began learning:

  • SQL and MySQL for data querying and database management
  • Power BI for dashboard development and business reporting
  • Tableau for interactive data visualization
  • Advanced Excel for reporting and data manipulation
  • Python for data analysis and automation

I also started working on hands-on projects using real-world datasets to strengthen my practical experience.

Current Learning Path

I am currently pursuing a Diploma in Data Analytics & AI Specialist, where I continue to expand my knowledge of analytics, business intelligence, and emerging AI technologies.

Looking Ahead

My goal is to combine my business experience and analytical skills to help organizations make informed decisions through data. As I continue learning and building projects, I look forward to applying data analytics to solve real-world business challenges and create meaningful impact.
description: How my experience in pension administration inspired my transition into data analytics."
title: My Journey into Data Analytics

Emoji can be enabled in a Hugo project in a number of ways. :zap:

Emoji Support

The emojify function can be called directly in templates or Inline Shortcodes.

To enable emoji globally, set enableEmoji to true in your site’s configuration and then you can type emoji shorthand codes directly in content files; e.g.

The Emoji cheat sheet is a useful reference for emoji shorthand codes.

N.B. The above steps enable Unicode Standard emoji characters and sequences in Hugo, however the rendering of these glyphs depends on the browser and the platform. To style the emoji you can either use a third party emoji font or a font stack; e.g.

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}
title: Emoji Support

content:
5 SQL Queries Every Data Analyst Should Know

Transition Into Analytics

SQL is one of the most important skills for data analysts. Whether you’re cleaning data, generating reports, or answering business questions, SQL helps transform raw data into meaningful insights.

As I transitioned into data analytics, SQL became one of the first technical skills I focused on learning. While SQL contains many advanced features, there are a few fundamental queries that analysts use regularly in real-world projects.

In this post, I’ll cover five SQL queries every data analyst should know and explain why they are important.

1. Filtering Data with WHERE

The WHERE clause allows analysts to retrieve only the data relevant to a specific question.

Example

SELECT *
FROM employees
WHERE department = 'Sales';

Why It Matters

In real-world datasets, analysts rarely need every record. Filtering helps focus on a specific customer segment, product category, region, or time period.

Common use cases include:

  • Finding customers from a specific city
  • Analyzing a particular product line
  • Reviewing transactions within a date range

2. Summarizing Data with GROUP BY

The GROUP BY clause is used to aggregate data and calculate metrics such as totals, averages, and counts.

Example

SELECT department,
       COUNT(*) AS employee_count
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;

Why It Matters

Business leaders often need summarized information rather than individual records.

Examples include:

  • Total sales by region
  • Number of customers by country
  • Average salary by department

Grouping data helps reveal patterns that are difficult to identify from raw rows alone.

3. Combining Tables with JOIN

Data is often stored across multiple tables. The JOIN statement allows analysts to combine related information.

Example

SELECT c.customer_name,
       o.order_date,
       o.order_amount
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o
ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;

Why It Matters

Most business databases are relational.

A typical analysis may require combining:

  • Customers and Orders
  • Employees and Departments
  • Products and Sales

Understanding joins is essential because valuable insights often exist across multiple datasets rather than within a single table.

4. Finding Top Results with ORDER BY and LIMIT

Analysts frequently need to identify the highest or lowest values in a dataset.

Example

SELECT product_name,
       total_sales
FROM products
ORDER BY total_sales DESC
LIMIT 5;

Why It Matters

This query helps answer questions such as:

  • What are the top-selling products?
  • Which customers generate the most revenue?
  • Which regions have the highest sales?

Ranking data is a common requirement in dashboards and management reports.

5. Using Aggregate Functions

Aggregate functions help summarize large amounts of data quickly.

Example

SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    SUM(order_amount) AS total_revenue,
    AVG(order_amount) AS average_order_value
FROM orders;

Common Aggregate Functions

Function Purpose
COUNT() Counts records
SUM() Calculates totals
AVG() Calculates averages
MIN() Finds lowest value
MAX() Finds highest value

Why It Matters

Organizations rely on KPIs and summary metrics to make decisions. Aggregate functions provide the foundation for many business reports and dashboards.

Putting It All Together

In practice, analysts often combine these concepts.

Example

SELECT region,
       COUNT(*) AS customers,
       SUM(sales_amount) AS total_sales
FROM sales
WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY total_sales DESC;

This single query:

  • Filters data
  • Groups records
  • Calculates totals
  • Sorts results

These are the exact skills analysts use daily when working with business data.

Key Takeaways

The five SQL concepts every data analyst should master are:

  1. WHERE – Filter data
  2. GROUP BY – Summarize data
  3. JOIN – Combine tables
  4. ORDER BY and LIMIT – Rank results
  5. Aggregate Functions – Calculate KPIs

While SQL offers many advanced capabilities, these fundamentals form the foundation of effective data analysis. Mastering them will make it easier to explore data, answer business questions, and build meaningful insights.

Conclusion

Learning SQL has been an important part of my transition into data analytics. The more I work with data, the more I appreciate how powerful SQL is for extracting information and supporting data-driven decisions.

For aspiring analysts, focusing on these five core query types is a great starting point. Once these concepts become second nature, you’ll be well prepared to tackle more advanced analytical challenges.

SQL is one of the most important skills for data analysts. Whether you’re cleaning data, generating reports, or answering business questions, SQL helps transform raw data into meaningful insights.

As I transitioned into data analytics, SQL became one of the first technical skills I focused on learning. While SQL contains many advanced features, there are a few fundamental queries that analysts use regularly in real-world projects.

In this post, I’ll cover five SQL queries every data analyst should know and explain why they are important.

1. Filtering Data with WHERE

The WHERE clause allows analysts to retrieve only the data relevant to a specific question.

Example

SELECT *
FROM employees
WHERE department = 'Sales';

Why It Matters

In real-world datasets, analysts rarely need every record. Filtering helps focus on a specific customer segment, product category, region, or time period.

Common use cases include:

  • Finding customers from a specific city
  • Analyzing a particular product line
  • Reviewing transactions within a date range

2. Summarizing Data with GROUP BY

The GROUP BY clause is used to aggregate data and calculate metrics such as totals, averages, and counts.

Example

SELECT department,
       COUNT(*) AS employee_count
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;

Why It Matters

Business leaders often need summarized information rather than individual records.

Examples include:

  • Total sales by region
  • Number of customers by country
  • Average salary by department

Grouping data helps reveal patterns that are difficult to identify from raw rows alone.

3. Combining Tables with JOIN

Data is often stored across multiple tables. The JOIN statement allows analysts to combine related information.

Example

SELECT c.customer_name,
       o.order_date,
       o.order_amount
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o
ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;

Why It Matters

Most business databases are relational.

A typical analysis may require combining:

  • Customers and Orders
  • Employees and Departments
  • Products and Sales

Understanding joins is essential because valuable insights often exist across multiple datasets rather than within a single table.

4. Finding Top Results with ORDER BY and LIMIT

Analysts frequently need to identify the highest or lowest values in a dataset.

Example

SELECT product_name,
       total_sales
FROM products
ORDER BY total_sales DESC
LIMIT 5;

Why It Matters

This query helps answer questions such as:

  • What are the top-selling products?
  • Which customers generate the most revenue?
  • Which regions have the highest sales?

Ranking data is a common requirement in dashboards and management reports.

5. Using Aggregate Functions

Aggregate functions help summarize large amounts of data quickly.

Example

SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    SUM(order_amount) AS total_revenue,
    AVG(order_amount) AS average_order_value
FROM orders;

Common Aggregate Functions

Function Purpose
COUNT() Counts records
SUM() Calculates totals
AVG() Calculates averages
MIN() Finds lowest value
MAX() Finds highest value

Why It Matters

Organizations rely on KPIs and summary metrics to make decisions. Aggregate functions provide the foundation for many business reports and dashboards.

Putting It All Together

In practice, analysts often combine these concepts.

Example

SELECT region,
       COUNT(*) AS customers,
       SUM(sales_amount) AS total_sales
FROM sales
WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY total_sales DESC;

This single query:

  • Filters data
  • Groups records
  • Calculates totals
  • Sorts results

These are the exact skills analysts use daily when working with business data.

Key Takeaways

The five SQL concepts every data analyst should master are:

  1. WHERE – Filter data
  2. GROUP BY – Summarize data
  3. JOIN – Combine tables
  4. ORDER BY and LIMIT – Rank results
  5. Aggregate Functions – Calculate KPIs

While SQL offers many advanced capabilities, these fundamentals form the foundation of effective data analysis. Mastering them will make it easier to explore data, answer business questions, and build meaningful insights.

Conclusion

Learning SQL has been an important part of my transition into data analytics. The more I work with data, the more I appreciate how powerful SQL is for extracting information and supporting data-driven decisions.

For aspiring analysts, focusing on these five core query types is a great starting point. Once these concepts become second nature, you’ll be well prepared to tackle more advanced analytical challenges.

Emoji Support

The emojify function can be called directly in templates or Inline Shortcodes.

To enable emoji globally, set enableEmoji to true in your site’s configuration and then you can type emoji shorthand codes directly in content files; e.g.

The Emoji cheat sheet is a useful reference for emoji shorthand codes.

N.B. The above steps enable Unicode Standard emoji characters and sequences in Hugo, however the rendering of these glyphs depends on the browser and the platform. To style the emoji you can either use a third party emoji font or a font stack; e.g.

  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}

content:
First Step Towards Visualization

Creating my first Power BI dashboard was an exciting milestone in my data analytics journey. It helped me turn raw data into meaningful insights and taught me how powerful visualization can be in storytelling. Building this dashboard was an eye‑opening experience into how global economic shifts impact companies, industries, and entire regions.

1. Project Overview

I built a World Layoffs - Insignts & Trends dashboard to analyze worldwide layoffs from 2020 to 2024, covering more than 1,000 companies, 31 industries, and nearly 485,000 employees affected. The goal of this project was to understand how layoffs evolved globally during and after the pandemic. I wanted to explore:

  • Which companies had the highest layoffs
  • Which industries were most affected
  • How layoffs changed year‑over‑year
  • Which regions experienced the largest impact
  • Monthly patterns and deep‑dive trends

Tool Used: Power BI Desktop
Dataset: Global Layoffs (2020–2024) — includes company details, industry, country, layoff counts, percentage laid off, funds raised, and timeline fields

2. Data Preparation

Before creating visuals, I performed data cleaning and transformation using Power Query Editor, ensuring accuracy and consistancy before modeling.

Key Steps Included:

  • Removing duplicates and null values
  • Standardizing industry and country names
  • Converting date fields into proper formats
  • Creating Year, Month, and Quarter columns
  • Adding calculated fields for YoY change and total layoffs
  • Ensuring numeric fields (layoff count, funds raised) were properly typed

3. Dashboard Design

I designed a two‑page dashboard to separate high‑level insights from deeper analysis.

Page 1: Global Layoff Overview

Focused on global patterns and top‑level metrics.

Key visuals:

  • KPI Cards: Total layoffs, average layoff %, max single-company layoff
  • Top 10 Companies by Layoffs
  • Industry‑Wise Layoffs (Pie Chart)
  • Yearly Totals + YoY Change
  • Global Map (Layoffs by Country)

Page 2: Detailed Layoff Insights & Deep Dive

This page explores detailed patterns and company‑level insights.

Key visuals:

  • Companies with 100% Layoffs (shutdowns)
  • Layoffs by Country & Industry (stacked bar)
  • Monthly Layoffs Trend (2020–2024)

My goal was a clean, intuitive layout that allows users to move from a global view to detailed insights seamlessly.

4. Key Insights

Tech and Retail were the hardest hit

Tech companies like Amazon, Tesla, Dell, Google, Meta, and SAP dominated the Top 10 list. Retail and Transportation also showed significant layoffs due to shifting consumer behavior and supply chain disruptions.

Layoffs peaked in 2023

The YoY chart revealed a sharp spike in 2023 — the highest in the dataset. 2024 showed early signs of stabilization.

Several companies shut down completely

The “100% Layoffs” table highlighted companies that ceased operations, including:

  • Katerra (Construction)
  • Bitwise (Tech)
  • Zulily (Retail)
  • Convoy (Logistics)

These cases often involved startups with high fundraising but unsustainable cost structures.

North America and Europe saw the largest impact

The map visualization showed the highest concentration of layoffs in the US, Canada, UK, and Germany.

Monthly trends showed seasonal patterns

Layoffs often spiked in Q1, aligning with annual restructuring cycles.

5. Challenges Faced

Like any real‑world project, this one came with its own learning curve:

  • Understanding how to model multiple dimensions (Company, Industry, Country, Date)
  • Choosing visuals that clearly communicate trends
  • Designing a layout that works across two pages
  • Syncing slicers across pages for a smooth user experience
  • Ensuring the dashboard remained clean despite large amounts of data

Each challenge helped me improve my BI design and analytical thinking.

6. What I Learned

This project strengthened my skills in:

  • Data cleaning and transformation using Power Query
  • Data modeling with a star schema
  • DAX calculations for YoY metrics and KPIs
  • Dashboard storytelling — turning raw data into meaningful insights
  • Designing multi‑page Power BI reports
  • Creating interactive visuals that support deeper exploration

Most importantly, I learned how to structure a dashboard that feels professional, intuitive, and portfolio‑ready.

Final Thoughts

The World Layoffs dashboard helped me understand how global events ripple across industries and regions. It also allowed me to demonstrate my ability to work with complex datasets, build insightful visuals, and communicate findings clearly.

This project reflects my passion for data storytelling and my commitment to building dashboards that deliver real value.
title: Building My First Power BI Dashboard

content:
Introduction

My journey into data analytics began while working in pension administration, where I was responsible for financial data reconciliation, reporting, and validating pension payment records. Although accuracy and compliance were key aspects of the role, I found myself particularly interested in analyzing data, identifying discrepancies, and uncovering patterns.

Discovering Analytics

A significant part of my work involved comparing datasets from banks against internal records, performing variance analysis in Excel, and preparing reports for management. These responsibilities strengthened my analytical thinking and attention to detail while showing me the value of data-driven decision-making.

Over time, I realized I wanted to deepen my analytical skills and work with more advanced tools that could transform raw data into actionable insights.

Building Technical Skills

To support this transition, I began learning:

  • SQL and MySQL for data querying and database management
  • Power BI for dashboard development and business reporting
  • Tableau for interactive data visualization
  • Advanced Excel for reporting and data manipulation
  • Python for data analysis and automation

I also started working on hands-on projects using real-world datasets to strengthen my practical experience.

Current Learning Path

I am currently pursuing a Diploma in Data Analytics & AI Specialist, where I continue to expand my knowledge of analytics, business intelligence, and emerging AI technologies.

Looking Ahead

My goal is to combine my business experience and analytical skills to help organizations make informed decisions through data. As I continue learning and building projects, I look forward to applying data analytics to solve real-world business challenges and create meaningful impact.
description: How my experience in pension administration inspired my transition into data analytics."
title: My Journey into Data Analytics

Hugo ships with several Embedded Shortcodes for rich content, along with a Privacy Config and a set of Simple Shortcodes that enable static and no-JS versions of various social media embeds.

X Simple Shortcode

{{< x user="GoHugoIO" id="1315233626070503424" >}}

Profile is a customizable single-page portfolio theme for Hugo featuring a contact form and blog. Made by @gurusabarishh.https://t.co/KwYcwfdy16

— GoHugo.io (@GoHugoIO) October 11, 2020

Vimeo Simple Shortcode

{{< vimeo 146022717 >}}


Youtube Simple Shortcode

{{< youtube w7Ft2ymGmfc >}}


Theme Custom Shortcodes

These shortcodes are not Hugo built-ins, but are provided by the theme.

Responsive Images with Cloudinary

You can learn more about this here.

Set the cloudinary_cloud_name parameter in your site config to use this shortcode.

{{< dynamic-img src="/my/image/on/cloudinary" title="A title for the image" >}}

Note that you do not include the file extension (e.g. .png) in the src parameter, as the shortcode will automatically determine the best quality and format for the user’s device.

Optionally, you can customize the general CSS styles for the image:

{{< dynamic-img src="/my/image/on/cloudinary" title="A title for the image" style="max-width:60%" >}}

title: Rich Content

content:
Transition Into Analytics

SQL is one of the most important skills for data analysts. Whether you’re cleaning data, generating reports, or answering business questions, SQL helps transform raw data into meaningful insights.

As I transitioned into data analytics, SQL became one of the first technical skills I focused on learning. While SQL contains many advanced features, there are a few fundamental queries that analysts use regularly in real-world projects.

In this post, I’ll cover five SQL queries every data analyst should know and explain why they are important.

1. Filtering Data with WHERE

The WHERE clause allows analysts to retrieve only the data relevant to a specific question.

Example
SELECT *
FROM employees
WHERE department = 'Sales';

Why It Matters

In real-world datasets, analysts rarely need every record. Filtering helps focus on a specific customer segment, product category, region, or time period.

Common use cases include:

  • Finding customers from a specific city
  • Analyzing a particular product line
  • Reviewing transactions within a date range

2. Summarizing Data with GROUP BY

The GROUP BY clause is used to aggregate data and calculate metrics such as totals, averages, and counts.

Example

SELECT department,
       COUNT(*) AS employee_count
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;

Why It Matters

Business leaders often need summarized information rather than individual records.

Examples include:

  • Total sales by region
  • Number of customers by country
  • Average salary by department

Grouping data helps reveal patterns that are difficult to identify from raw rows alone.

3. Combining Tables with JOIN

Data is often stored across multiple tables. The JOIN statement allows analysts to combine related information.

Example

SELECT c.customer_name,
       o.order_date,
       o.order_amount
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o
ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;

Why It Matters

Most business databases are relational.

A typical analysis may require combining:

  • Customers and Orders
  • Employees and Departments
  • Products and Sales

Understanding joins is essential because valuable insights often exist across multiple datasets rather than within a single table.

4. Finding Top Results with ORDER BY and LIMIT

Analysts frequently need to identify the highest or lowest values in a dataset.

Example

SELECT product_name,
       total_sales
FROM products
ORDER BY total_sales DESC
LIMIT 5;

Why It Matters

This query helps answer questions such as:

  • What are the top-selling products?
  • Which customers generate the most revenue?
  • Which regions have the highest sales?

Ranking data is a common requirement in dashboards and management reports.

5. Using Aggregate Functions

Aggregate functions help summarize large amounts of data quickly.

Example

SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    SUM(order_amount) AS total_revenue,
    AVG(order_amount) AS average_order_value
FROM orders;

Common Aggregate Functions

Function Purpose
COUNT() Counts records
SUM() Calculates totals
AVG() Calculates averages
MIN() Finds lowest value
MAX() Finds highest value

Why It Matters

Organizations rely on KPIs and summary metrics to make decisions. Aggregate functions provide the foundation for many business reports and dashboards.

Putting It All Together

In practice, analysts often combine these concepts.

Example

SELECT region,
       COUNT(*) AS customers,
       SUM(sales_amount) AS total_sales
FROM sales
WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY total_sales DESC;

This single query:

  • Filters data
  • Groups records
  • Calculates totals
  • Sorts results

These are the exact skills analysts use daily when working with business data.

Key Takeaways

The five SQL concepts every data analyst should master are:

  1. WHERE – Filter data
  2. GROUP BY – Summarize data
  3. JOIN – Combine tables
  4. ORDER BY and LIMIT – Rank results
  5. Aggregate Functions – Calculate KPIs

While SQL offers many advanced capabilities, these fundamentals form the foundation of effective data analysis. Mastering them will make it easier to explore data, answer business questions, and build meaningful insights.

Conclusion

Learning SQL has been an important part of my transition into data analytics. The more I work with data, the more I appreciate how powerful SQL is for extracting information and supporting data-driven decisions.

For aspiring analysts, focusing on these five core query types is a great starting point. Once these concepts become second nature, you’ll be well prepared to tackle more advanced analytical challenges.

The emojify function can be called directly in templates or Inline Shortcodes.

To enable emoji globally, set enableEmoji to true in your site’s configuration and then you can type emoji shorthand codes directly in content files; e.g.

The Emoji cheat sheet is a useful reference for emoji shorthand codes.

N.B. The above steps enable Unicode Standard emoji characters and sequences in Hugo, however the rendering of these glyphs depends on the browser and the platform. To style the emoji you can either use a third party emoji font or a font stack; e.g.

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}

content:
First Step Towards Visualization

Creating my first Power BI dashboard was an exciting milestone in my data analytics journey. It helped me turn raw data into meaningful insights and taught me how powerful visualization can be in storytelling. Building this dashboard was an eye‑opening experience into how global economic shifts impact companies, industries, and entire regions.

1. Project Overview

I built a World Layoffs - Insignts & Trends dashboard to analyze worldwide layoffs from 2020 to 2024, covering more than 1,000 companies, 31 industries, and nearly 485,000 employees affected. The goal of this project was to understand how layoffs evolved globally during and after the pandemic. I wanted to explore:

  • Which companies had the highest layoffs
  • Which industries were most affected
  • How layoffs changed year‑over‑year
  • Which regions experienced the largest impact
  • Monthly patterns and deep‑dive trends

Tool Used: Power BI Desktop
Dataset: Global Layoffs (2020–2024) — includes company details, industry, country, layoff counts, percentage laid off, funds raised, and timeline fields

2. Data Preparation

Before creating visuals, I performed data cleaning and transformation using Power Query Editor, ensuring accuracy and consistancy before modeling.

Key Steps Included:

  • Removing duplicates and null values
  • Standardizing industry and country names
  • Converting date fields into proper formats
  • Creating Year, Month, and Quarter columns
  • Adding calculated fields for YoY change and total layoffs
  • Ensuring numeric fields (layoff count, funds raised) were properly typed

3. Dashboard Design

I designed a two‑page dashboard to separate high‑level insights from deeper analysis.

Page 1: Global Layoff Overview

Focused on global patterns and top‑level metrics.

Key visuals:

  • KPI Cards: Total layoffs, average layoff %, max single-company layoff
  • Top 10 Companies by Layoffs
  • Industry‑Wise Layoffs (Pie Chart)
  • Yearly Totals + YoY Change
  • Global Map (Layoffs by Country)

Page 2: Detailed Layoff Insights & Deep Dive

This page explores detailed patterns and company‑level insights.

Key visuals:

  • Companies with 100% Layoffs (shutdowns)
  • Layoffs by Country & Industry (stacked bar)
  • Monthly Layoffs Trend (2020–2024)

My goal was a clean, intuitive layout that allows users to move from a global view to detailed insights seamlessly.

4. Key Insights

Tech and Retail were the hardest hit

Tech companies like Amazon, Tesla, Dell, Google, Meta, and SAP dominated the Top 10 list. Retail and Transportation also showed significant layoffs due to shifting consumer behavior and supply chain disruptions.

Layoffs peaked in 2023

The YoY chart revealed a sharp spike in 2023 — the highest in the dataset. 2024 showed early signs of stabilization.

Several companies shut down completely

The “100% Layoffs” table highlighted companies that ceased operations, including:

  • Katerra (Construction)
  • Bitwise (Tech)
  • Zulily (Retail)
  • Convoy (Logistics)

These cases often involved startups with high fundraising but unsustainable cost structures.

North America and Europe saw the largest impact

The map visualization showed the highest concentration of layoffs in the US, Canada, UK, and Germany.

Monthly trends showed seasonal patterns

Layoffs often spiked in Q1, aligning with annual restructuring cycles.

5. Challenges Faced

Like any real‑world project, this one came with its own learning curve:

  • Understanding how to model multiple dimensions (Company, Industry, Country, Date)
  • Choosing visuals that clearly communicate trends
  • Designing a layout that works across two pages
  • Syncing slicers across pages for a smooth user experience
  • Ensuring the dashboard remained clean despite large amounts of data

Each challenge helped me improve my BI design and analytical thinking.

6. What I Learned

This project strengthened my skills in:

  • Data cleaning and transformation using Power Query
  • Data modeling with a star schema
  • DAX calculations for YoY metrics and KPIs
  • Dashboard storytelling — turning raw data into meaningful insights
  • Designing multi‑page Power BI reports
  • Creating interactive visuals that support deeper exploration

Most importantly, I learned how to structure a dashboard that feels professional, intuitive, and portfolio‑ready.

Final Thoughts

The World Layoffs dashboard helped me understand how global events ripple across industries and regions. It also allowed me to demonstrate my ability to work with complex datasets, build insightful visuals, and communicate findings clearly.

This project reflects my passion for data storytelling and my commitment to building dashboards that deliver real value.
title: Building My First Power BI Dashboard

content:
Introduction

My journey into data analytics began while working in pension administration, where I was responsible for financial data reconciliation, reporting, and validating pension payment records. Although accuracy and compliance were key aspects of the role, I found myself particularly interested in analyzing data, identifying discrepancies, and uncovering patterns.

Discovering Analytics

A significant part of my work involved comparing datasets from banks against internal records, performing variance analysis in Excel, and preparing reports for management. These responsibilities strengthened my analytical thinking and attention to detail while showing me the value of data-driven decision-making.

Over time, I realized I wanted to deepen my analytical skills and work with more advanced tools that could transform raw data into actionable insights.

Building Technical Skills

To support this transition, I began learning:

  • SQL and MySQL for data querying and database management
  • Power BI for dashboard development and business reporting
  • Tableau for interactive data visualization
  • Advanced Excel for reporting and data manipulation
  • Python for data analysis and automation

I also started working on hands-on projects using real-world datasets to strengthen my practical experience.

Current Learning Path

I am currently pursuing a Diploma in Data Analytics & AI Specialist, where I continue to expand my knowledge of analytics, business intelligence, and emerging AI technologies.

Looking Ahead

My goal is to combine my business experience and analytical skills to help organizations make informed decisions through data. As I continue learning and building projects, I look forward to applying data analytics to solve real-world business challenges and create meaningful impact.
description: How my experience in pension administration inspired my transition into data analytics."
title: My Journey into Data Analytics

title: Rich Content

content:
Transition Into Analytics

SQL is one of the most important skills for data analysts. Whether you’re cleaning data, generating reports, or answering business questions, SQL helps transform raw data into meaningful insights.

As I transitioned into data analytics, SQL became one of the first technical skills I focused on learning. While SQL contains many advanced features, there are a few fundamental queries that analysts use regularly in real-world projects.

In this post, I’ll cover five SQL queries every data analyst should know and explain why they are important.

1. Filtering Data with WHERE

The WHERE clause allows analysts to retrieve only the data relevant to a specific question.

Example
SELECT *
FROM employees
WHERE department = 'Sales';
Why It Matters

In real-world datasets, analysts rarely need every record. Filtering helps focus on a specific customer segment, product category, region, or time period.

Common use cases include:

  • Finding customers from a specific city
  • Analyzing a particular product line
  • Reviewing transactions within a date range

2. Summarizing Data with GROUP BY

The GROUP BY clause is used to aggregate data and calculate metrics such as totals, averages, and counts.

Example
SELECT department,
       COUNT(*) AS employee_count
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;
Why It Matters

Business leaders often need summarized information rather than individual records.

Examples include:

  • Total sales by region
  • Number of customers by country
  • Average salary by department

Grouping data helps reveal patterns that are difficult to identify from raw rows alone.

3. Combining Tables with JOIN

Data is often stored across multiple tables. The JOIN statement allows analysts to combine related information.

Example

SELECT c.customer_name,
       o.order_date,
       o.order_amount
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o
ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;

Why It Matters

Most business databases are relational.

A typical analysis may require combining:

  • Customers and Orders
  • Employees and Departments
  • Products and Sales

Understanding joins is essential because valuable insights often exist across multiple datasets rather than within a single table.

4. Finding Top Results with ORDER BY and LIMIT

Analysts frequently need to identify the highest or lowest values in a dataset.

Example

SELECT product_name,
       total_sales
FROM products
ORDER BY total_sales DESC
LIMIT 5;

Why It Matters

This query helps answer questions such as:

  • What are the top-selling products?
  • Which customers generate the most revenue?
  • Which regions have the highest sales?

Ranking data is a common requirement in dashboards and management reports.

5. Using Aggregate Functions

Aggregate functions help summarize large amounts of data quickly.

Example

SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    SUM(order_amount) AS total_revenue,
    AVG(order_amount) AS average_order_value
FROM orders;

Common Aggregate Functions

Function Purpose
COUNT() Counts records
SUM() Calculates totals
AVG() Calculates averages
MIN() Finds lowest value
MAX() Finds highest value

Why It Matters

Organizations rely on KPIs and summary metrics to make decisions. Aggregate functions provide the foundation for many business reports and dashboards.

Putting It All Together

In practice, analysts often combine these concepts.

Example

SELECT region,
       COUNT(*) AS customers,
       SUM(sales_amount) AS total_sales
FROM sales
WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY total_sales DESC;

This single query:

  • Filters data
  • Groups records
  • Calculates totals
  • Sorts results

These are the exact skills analysts use daily when working with business data.

Key Takeaways

The five SQL concepts every data analyst should master are:

  1. WHERE – Filter data
  2. GROUP BY – Summarize data
  3. JOIN – Combine tables
  4. ORDER BY and LIMIT – Rank results
  5. Aggregate Functions – Calculate KPIs

While SQL offers many advanced capabilities, these fundamentals form the foundation of effective data analysis. Mastering them will make it easier to explore data, answer business questions, and build meaningful insights.

Conclusion

Learning SQL has been an important part of my transition into data analytics. The more I work with data, the more I appreciate how powerful SQL is for extracting information and supporting data-driven decisions.

For aspiring analysts, focusing on these five core query types is a great starting point. Once these concepts become second nature, you’ll be well prepared to tackle more advanced analytical challenges.

Emoji can be enabled in a Hugo project in a number of ways. :zap:

Emoji Support

The emojify function can be called directly in templates or Inline Shortcodes.

To enable emoji globally, set enableEmoji to true in your site’s configuration and then you can type emoji shorthand codes directly in content files; e.g.

The Emoji cheat sheet is a useful reference for emoji shorthand codes.

N.B. The above steps enable Unicode Standard emoji characters and sequences in Hugo, however the rendering of these glyphs depends on the browser and the platform. To style the emoji you can either use a third party emoji font or a font stack; e.g.

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}

content:
First Step Towards Visualization

Creating my first Power BI dashboard was an exciting milestone in my data analytics journey. It helped me turn raw data into meaningful insights and taught me how powerful visualization can be in storytelling. Building this dashboard was an eye‑opening experience into how global economic shifts impact companies, industries, and entire regions.

1. Project Overview

I built a World Layoffs - Insignts & Trends dashboard to analyze worldwide layoffs from 2020 to 2024, covering more than 1,000 companies, 31 industries, and nearly 485,000 employees affected. The goal of this project was to understand how layoffs evolved globally during and after the pandemic. I wanted to explore:

  • Which companies had the highest layoffs
  • Which industries were most affected
  • How layoffs changed year‑over‑year
  • Which regions experienced the largest impact
  • Monthly patterns and deep‑dive trends

Tool Used: Power BI Desktop
Dataset: Global Layoffs (2020–2024) — includes company details, industry, country, layoff counts, percentage laid off, funds raised, and timeline fields

2. Data Preparation

Before creating visuals, I performed data cleaning and transformation using Power Query Editor, ensuring accuracy and consistancy before modeling.

Key Steps Included:

  • Removing duplicates and null values
  • Standardizing industry and country names
  • Converting date fields into proper formats
  • Creating Year, Month, and Quarter columns
  • Adding calculated fields for YoY change and total layoffs
  • Ensuring numeric fields (layoff count, funds raised) were properly typed

3. Dashboard Design

I designed a two‑page dashboard to separate high‑level insights from deeper analysis.

Page 1: Global Layoff Overview

Focused on global patterns and top‑level metrics.

Key visuals:

  • KPI Cards: Total layoffs, average layoff %, max single-company layoff
  • Top 10 Companies by Layoffs
  • Industry‑Wise Layoffs (Pie Chart)
  • Yearly Totals + YoY Change
  • Global Map (Layoffs by Country)

Page 2: Detailed Layoff Insights & Deep Dive

This page explores detailed patterns and company‑level insights.

Key visuals:

  • Companies with 100% Layoffs (shutdowns)
  • Layoffs by Country & Industry (stacked bar)
  • Monthly Layoffs Trend (2020–2024)

My goal was a clean, intuitive layout that allows users to move from a global view to detailed insights seamlessly.

4. Key Insights

Tech and Retail were the hardest hit

Tech companies like Amazon, Tesla, Dell, Google, Meta, and SAP dominated the Top 10 list. Retail and Transportation also showed significant layoffs due to shifting consumer behavior and supply chain disruptions.

Layoffs peaked in 2023

The YoY chart revealed a sharp spike in 2023 — the highest in the dataset. 2024 showed early signs of stabilization.

Several companies shut down completely

The “100% Layoffs” table highlighted companies that ceased operations, including:

  • Katerra (Construction)
  • Bitwise (Tech)
  • Zulily (Retail)
  • Convoy (Logistics)

These cases often involved startups with high fundraising but unsustainable cost structures.

North America and Europe saw the largest impact

The map visualization showed the highest concentration of layoffs in the US, Canada, UK, and Germany.

Monthly trends showed seasonal patterns

Layoffs often spiked in Q1, aligning with annual restructuring cycles.

5. Challenges Faced

Like any real‑world project, this one came with its own learning curve:

  • Understanding how to model multiple dimensions (Company, Industry, Country, Date)
  • Choosing visuals that clearly communicate trends
  • Designing a layout that works across two pages
  • Syncing slicers across pages for a smooth user experience
  • Ensuring the dashboard remained clean despite large amounts of data

Each challenge helped me improve my BI design and analytical thinking.

6. What I Learned

This project strengthened my skills in:

  • Data cleaning and transformation using Power Query
  • Data modeling with a star schema
  • DAX calculations for YoY metrics and KPIs
  • Dashboard storytelling — turning raw data into meaningful insights
  • Designing multi‑page Power BI reports
  • Creating interactive visuals that support deeper exploration

Most importantly, I learned how to structure a dashboard that feels professional, intuitive, and portfolio‑ready.

Final Thoughts

The World Layoffs dashboard helped me understand how global events ripple across industries and regions. It also allowed me to demonstrate my ability to work with complex datasets, build insightful visuals, and communicate findings clearly.

This project reflects my passion for data storytelling and my commitment to building dashboards that deliver real value.
title: Building My First Power BI Dashboard

content:
Introduction

My journey into data analytics began while working in pension administration, where I was responsible for financial data reconciliation, reporting, and validating pension payment records. Although accuracy and compliance were key aspects of the role, I found myself particularly interested in analyzing data, identifying discrepancies, and uncovering patterns.

Discovering Analytics

A significant part of my work involved comparing datasets from banks against internal records, performing variance analysis in Excel, and preparing reports for management. These responsibilities strengthened my analytical thinking and attention to detail while showing me the value of data-driven decision-making.

Over time, I realized I wanted to deepen my analytical skills and work with more advanced tools that could transform raw data into actionable insights.

Building Technical Skills

To support this transition, I began learning:

  • SQL and MySQL for data querying and database management
  • Power BI for dashboard development and business reporting
  • Tableau for interactive data visualization
  • Advanced Excel for reporting and data manipulation
  • Python for data analysis and automation

I also started working on hands-on projects using real-world datasets to strengthen my practical experience.

Current Learning Path

I am currently pursuing a Diploma in Data Analytics & AI Specialist, where I continue to expand my knowledge of analytics, business intelligence, and emerging AI technologies.

Looking Ahead

My goal is to combine my business experience and analytical skills to help organizations make informed decisions through data. As I continue learning and building projects, I look forward to applying data analytics to solve real-world business challenges and create meaningful impact.
description: How my experience in pension administration inspired my transition into data analytics."
title: My Journey into Data Analytics
